sentences.text: Yes.
OK, I have discharge papers from the labor camp, but these discharge papers don't mean too much to certain-- depending who looks at it.
You may find sometimes an officer or someone who was-- wants your ID, and I would say nothing, say that it doesn't mean nothing, may tear it-- may even tear it.
They don't even have to read, some Hungarians.
They don't know, maybe they say we have stolen it.
So, I want to be in Budapest again.
And again, what's the story?
I got to wait, not to be caught there.
And where am I going to live?
In the Jewish quarters I have only someone I know, but in other places I don't have.
So finally, I go and find myself a place in [NON-ENGLISH] street number three, with a non-Jew.
And I explain the whole story.
I'm in.
She accepts me.
I have all in with the police.
And this is a safe place already, because in this house probably they're not coming.
Finally, one night, they come here also, and they find me.
But however, there were also living there a Hungarian, her husband-- she was retired, her husband died.
Her husband was a colonel or something.
And she begged them, leave all this.
You leave him alone.
So they left me alone.
And so I was in this place for quite a while.
Finally, I'm everyday going, struggling, seeing whether I can go from here, where I can go.
And no place, no way I can get out from Hungary or go.
So I didn't-- still didn't give up.
I figured I'd go back home and see what I can do home.
How did I make a living?
It's a whole story.
I became like a dealer in lumber there, helping out financial.
In the meantime, from Jews in the Carpathian were taking away trades from everyone-- stores were taken away and were given to non-Jews, who are Hungarians, who came from Hungary.
There's all a story, and Jews are becoming very, in the Carpathian, very dependent on each other.
And they don't have incomes anymore, they were taking all the trades.
My father's trade wasn't taken, because the only one was a tinsmith.
So I stayed home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At this time, there was still somehow chance some, very few, escaped into the Soviet Union.
Because the Soviet Union was bordering, at that time, the Carpathian.
I was considered also one time to go away, but that was also almost impossibility.
If you were caught, you're escaping the gendarme, you couldn't escape just like this.
If somehow you lived nearby that town-- so once I was trying there to go nearby town where my father was born to Turin, it's not far from the border.
And I told myself, look here, there's nothing to do.
I'll escape to the Soviet Union just to see over there it's a different story.
But as I came to Turin, there are police and gendarmes.
And I was advised that the Jews don't do it because many people came here and they asked questions.
Who are you?
And they know you are coming because you want to cross the border.
And besides big mountains, it was in the winter, you couldn't do that.
I was trying.
Maybe this will help.
So I come back.
I'm still again in Dovhoje.
I've been coming to the-- I'm in Dovhoje, staying for quite a time, going on and off the Budapest.
And trying again immigration, immigration.
But it came to a point where I saw myself that nothing will happen.
I can go through many things with my attempt was.
Many people are in labor camp.
Very few people are in Dovhoje, like me, already, because they're in labor camps.
My age are not there.
They are smaller, younger, or olders are 40.
They left their wives and their children.
And they are somewhere in labor camp.
They're still going on correspondence.
Maybe they can visit them.
But I'm only [INAUDIBLE].
And the gendarmes are there and looking there's a man there.
His age?
What happened?
Sometimes they want my ID.
So I show them all that I was from labor camp.
They [INAUDIBLE], they look at it.
They don't know why.
It's mystery.
But they've seen if I stayed here in Dovhoje, something sometime will happen to me.
The gendarmes are very harsh to everyone there, especially to Jews.
The rations is bread there, and they don't give to Jews.
They give only to non-Jews.
Comes the wars broke up with the Soviet Union.
This was in June 1941.
I'm going skipping very fast, because I want to come to more point.
And we are not bordering anymore.
The Soviet Union it's already we are boarding.
German occupies Soviet territory, 1941.
At one point, Hungarians are giving out new rules, new laws, anti-Jewish laws.
And one of the laws, which no one understood it-- as a matter of fact, I have it here, written out what it is.
But to a plain man it's hard to understand-- that Jews are required to prove their citizenship in the Carpathian.
These are considered Polish Jews, and they're not citizens.
Who needed a citizenship?
No one needed a citizenship.
No one was asking for a citizenship until an outside Jew and a woman [INAUDIBLE], yes, I want to go to America, needed to a citizenship or needed some papers.
And the rules are very harsh.
Rules are you've got to find your grandfather paid taxes for in 1867.
He was born that domicile.
I can read you about these rules, rules which a plain person it's impossible to-- And they give you six months.
That if in six months you still cannot proof your citizenship, you will be deported.
However, during the six months, we can give you a postponement, provided that you have some paper or want to apply for some paper.
And you go to the police station.
Police station had a sign, [NON-ENGLISH], not in your own town.
And over there, they give you a postponement of six months, otherwise you'll be deported.
But Jews in the Dovhoje and other places to say we're deported, we knowing what it means.
Who wants to deport?
Where to?
What for?
Who am I--
Now, the men are in the labor camps.
And the children are, and the women are no income.
So they take it very easy.
Nothing.
So at one point, the gendarmes interplay their laws and their own way to their discretion.
They take at random a Sunday morning, I was there in Dovhoje, and take some families, not with children because they know.
And take them away to a place, [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know, by bus they take them.
And finally from [NON-ENGLISH], we hear they took them away to [NON-ENGLISH] and other places.
And over there, they put them over the border to Poland.
It was already occupied Soviet territory, and there are Ukrainians, and there are Germans there.
I never knew about further what's happening, but we have started to see that this is a serious business already.
And they're going at random.
Some people could pay them off, and some not.
At random they take in the morning, at night.
Us, they didn't take.
So everyone started to look for paper where to get, what to get.
Everyone started.
Maybe you'll get a citizenship.
They were naive.
So were we naive.
It's almost impossible.
You go to an office.
Oh, on a birthday, you don't find your birthday.
They don't want to give it to you.
You have to stay.
Come next week.
Impossibility.
Finally, they started to deport.
And we don't know where their fate is.
Oh, but we hear already.
What's happening to someone they deported?
They wind up in [INAUDIBLE], and they're killing there.
We didn't want to believe their stories going on.
So they give now a story that belonging here for another three months or so months.
Only those who have prolonged already.
I, with my sister, go and start to make like this.
Who this-- those who that have prolonged, they put a stamp on their thing.
Who didn't have prolonged, they didn't have a stamp on their papers.
So we took and I went to [NON-ENGLISH], and I have prolonged through a bribery, prolonged some three or four papers.
We go home and take a hard boiled egg when it's hot, you know, and put it on the stamp and then stamp and transfer it on another paper.
They didn't know that we went by, showed that I have a stamp also.
About three, four week would do, but further we couldn't do.
So at least we're safe.
So deportations start-- 12,000 Jews were deported.
Not a once, little by little at random, mostly from the borders of Carpathian, because otherwise they had to take from other cities, better exiles.
They had too much work there to take them to different station, and to take him to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was the place not far from the border.
What about Dovhoje?
What happened in Dovhoje?
In Dovhoje they deported about four or five families of which we have proof.
Also they deported in Dovhoje several families.
A teacher, one was deported.
And another family, about three or four families was there.
So finally, the deportation was going on, and people didn't know what to do. 12,000 Jews were deported to the Soviet-occupied territories and were killed there.
I have some-- some were lucky and somehow smuggled back themselves to Hungary to [NON-ENGLISH].
Long stories.
I'm going to interview now 87-year-old man by the name of Morgan, who was there-- who, he was deported.
But some, finally, he somehow managed to come back to Hungary.
It's a long story.
I know it--
So very many were able single somehow to managed to come back under certain circumstances.
Stories, unbelievable.
So this is 1941.
About his deportation it's a lot, a lot of people-- taken by the Hungarians, by the Hungarian gendarme, by the Hungarian police, and mostly from the Carpathian.
They are not consider Jews.
And they were placed in-- through the border, through and through the border.
And over there was going on for a week, or two week, to further transfer that somewhere.
Finally, they find out that [INAUDIBLE] killed by the Germans, by the Ukraine, and so on.
No, no.
We  had in Dovhoje.
Because we had the papers.
In other words, we had-- ours was-- we had an extension.
Our papers had an exchange.
And so a few more people.
And they couldn't deport, because it was very difficult from Dovhoje.
There was no train.
They had to go by bus.
One time they deported an entire bus.
And I managed to go to the bus station and the bus driver that the bus had broken down.
He cannot go further.
So they couldn't transport them.
OK, no, that I wasn't deported at that point.
OK.
Now comes another story.
I'm evading constantly.
Everyone is labor camp.
And here is a  single man in Dovhoje-- people are looking, the non-Jews, especially the police.
And if they ever come-- they see a single man, a Jew, without any-- no, in Budapest you mingled over-- whether you are in labor camp or no.
There are many who are not still in labor camp who had all kinds of-- they had certain jobs.
And many Jews didn't consider them Jews.
They proved they're not Jews.
What story that goes on everyone has something, but most of the people in Hungary were in labor camps.
One another or this or that.
In a labor camp, from time to time they wrote home.
It was certainly labor camps also they came home.
What they did in labor camps different work, different stories, I cannot do.
Finally, they come to this stage.
I am avoiding constantly, because I see that despite they have a discharge, but this discharge was conditionally, and they have my number in Budapest.
And they have the thing.
And in Budapest, I go again.
And I have to go to the police station.
And I show my discharge and everything is all right.
But I live a street assuming 18.
I go down after I leave-- I lived on that street.
And when I came to them, I told 118.
Another way that they shouldn't be able to send them to-- the mail shouldn't come.
And many people did this, and they didn't know what it is.
Finally comes the story, evading, and evading, and evading.
In 1942, in May, it would be May around, toward end of May, it comes-- they were looking for me for a year and a half.
They couldn't find me no place.
Finally, came.
No-- I made else arrangement indoor with the post office.
I had an acquaintance who was giving out, whenever came such a mail.
And I'm getting all so such a-- now.
Usually they are getting someone who was evading a long time and couldn't the find them, they put on his draft, SAS-- S-A-S.
I don't know whether you have heard this?
SAS means it-- right away you have to come.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Rush-- daily rush.
It was known.
Now, here's a story.
If you for some reason evaded, or you came late, or something, you did something, they had also a labor camp they called [NON-ENGLISH]-- a punishment labor camp.
They put you in a place where, in other words, they knew that this labor camp is punished labor.
In other words, they did harsh things, because you evaded, you did things.
Now, everyone who has a SAS is considered that something must have been done that-- so in mine they put a SAS.
And I understood that they send me to some camp, but not necessarily everyone.
And here you must go, because if you not go, you had death threats.
I mean this is not that you evaded, you go.
So I decide, what can I do?
And right away, it was on a Saturday.
Saturday we don't travel.
I didn't have too much packages.
Now, here at this point, there are many people in Budapest who were big shots and evaded for some reasons.
They had acquaintances.
They knew how to manage, to manipulate.
They were not considered as Jews.
And this was an elite.
And they evaded for a long time sometimes.
They evaded labor camps.
And here come a story already.
The war broke up the Soviet occupied the Soviet.
And the Hungarian, according to the information, had to send 300,000 men to the front.
So they sent 200,000.
And 100,000 they want to send from a labor camp, Jews to where whatever it is.
And remember a labor camp is being considered as a unit of an army.
And we are in the war now.
You are considered as you are under the rules, like would be awarded traitor or whatever it is.
So finally, I am getting a letter to go to Vac, V-A-C, not far from Budapest.
Very nice town.
This in my place where I hope to do right away to come there.
And I'm going to Budapest.
I see in the train a lot of people are going, including myself, are going with knapsacks.
They were prepared already.
And most the-- they're accompanied by their wives, and by their children, ones who had children.
Young people, and you can see a [INAUDIBLE].
These were already-- they leave from Budapest, too.
And the last time, there are many, but now this is a unit of 200 people.
So being that I was drafted to Budapest, that's why I was assigned to a labor unit from Budapest.
So I find myself in this unit, coming to watch.
And I don't have a knapsack like everyone else.
And there are commanders, and soldiers, and guards.
Like in every day--
And alas, there were several labor camps, not in the same place were we were.
We were where the Jewish community were, and other places.
And these units are assigned also all to go to the Soviet occupied territory.
We know that everyone who goes to watch later will go to the Soviet occupied territory.
There is a-- was a commander by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], Hungarian.
He was like, not a colonel, I would say a [NON-ENGLISH]-- an officer.
He was in command of the whole town, a whole this operation.
And he was sending out every week one or two units.
He was a [NON-ENGLISH], like on a white horse.
So I'm coming there, doctor, lawyers, accountants, business people.
I find myself nothing.
Did nothing.
We are just in the beginning of labor camp.
They don't anything.
And there are a sergeant by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] who was in charge of all our unit of our 200 people.
A harsh man, a wild man, a drunk.
He was a first World War-- he was, what do you call it?
A soldier.
And he became now, not an officer-- he hardly can speak Hungarian, his own language.
He was employed in the city of Budapest as lighting up lamps in the park.
Oh, can imagine, he become-- not the commander, executor of-- and punishment, and going on are all kind, and there are more.
So questions are being asked in roll call.
Who was this?
Who was that?
Everyone was saying his profession.
So it comes to my profession, what am I going to say?
I'm a wood chopper.
A wood chopper is a-- this is a-- you know a lower grade and whatever could pass of wood-- a wood chopper.
Budapest wood chopper.
And I would chop.
I know how to cut wood.
A wood chopper was very nice to the sounded that there is among these people also a wood chopper.
They assign me to chop wood at the kitchen, which is a good job, because in the kitchen you don't go out to work.
So they're going out to work and--
I didn't have any other profession I'm going to say.
I was started a doctor.
I'm a student.
But I say a wood chopper.
Yeah, a useful laborer, a wood chopper.
And I have seen the man, I say a wood chopper.
There's not wood chopper there.
And I know how to chop wood, because it's--
So finally, a wood chopper.
And it's harsh things that's happening there.
Unbelievable beatings, because every day punishments, Jews, roll call, waking up at night.
They preparing they're going to the Soviet occupied territories to work there.
There are several units, people trying from there to buy out themselves.
This [NON-ENGLISH] was a smart man, and made every week a role call of all the units.
There are five, six units.
And already who bribed him from before, through his wife he knew already the names and had in front of him.
He didn't want to say that he was going to release those.
But somehow he manages [INAUDIBLE], who has got a five, who has got three children, who is poor.
I tried also to evade, but I wasn't successful.
But some who bribed him, for somehow he had the right to release them from the-- he had all the right.
So he released a few of them-- not to-- not to go to-- assign to another unit.
These were people from Budapest who are very influential, probably.
So finally comes the story.
It's long.
We are there three months in Vac, in labor camp.
Having this yellow band, having this cap.
One day they ship us.
They don't tell us where you go, but we understood they did.
I mean there's no-- you're going to this.
Put us on a train, not only us, a few more.
But each unit is different.
We are-- our [NON-ENGLISH], a [NON-ENGLISH] means a unit of 200 people.
It's here.
Now that [NON-ENGLISH] is at a different place.
Yeah, about I would say 500, 600, if not more.
Going for three days.
I have the map how we're going.
Now, we didn't have that time a map.
We didn't now, but today I know.
Finally, we wind up in Kiev, in the station, 1942, August somewhere.
Winding up in Kiev.
In Kiev he lines us up at the station.
And they are coming, officers greeting us.
And here comes Colonel Multajny.
We know now his name.
At that time, who knew his name.
Multajny was, in Kiev, in charge of the entire Hungarian unit.
He was a lieutenant colonel, a colonel, below a general.
At the station, big announcement with a loudspeaker.
They didn't have in Kiev, how it looked.
Everyone should line up.
He says in Hungarian-- I'm going to tell this in Hungarian, I'm going to translate.
[SPEAKING HUNGARIAN] You stinky Jew, from here never you will come out alive.
Here you will perish.
This was his greetings in Kiev, now I remember.
But now comes a different story.
Now comes everyone should take out his jewelery, or valuables, watches.
Someone has money, or someone-- and the-- what do you call the-- do they call it?
[NON-ENGLISH] Military police-- military police.
And they are going to look.
Now, they find someone has a good watch, take away.
Someone has a ring, takes it off.
Someone has something better, whatever.
I didn't have too much.
I didn't have from Dovhoje, nothing there.
And so they collected that all kind.
What happened to them?
This I don't know.
So about 600 people.
This was going on-- plain robbery.
Robbery, but who cares?
Robbery.
From there we go to a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means this was the Soviets probably had a place where soldiers were training, they take us to that place, all the group.
Our group of 200 is separated.
The other group is separated.
And I find myself in Kiev in the [NON-ENGLISH], in 1942.
And next day, they're taking people to work.
While they're going to taking people to work-- I mean the labor camp was restored-- they take them-- what you call it a place?
[HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian.
There was a place in Kiev where they were building carriages for the war.
From wood carriages, [HUNGARIAN].
And there were there Hungarian soldiers or Hungarian supervisors.
Unbelievable.
[NON-ENGLISH], they called it-- the engineer course.
And to work there, they are coming up beaten up with signs .
It's unheard, unbelievable.
I wasn't there.
And everyone is avoiding not the go to work there.
But you cannot.
In the morning they had roll call.
And they take out 50 people, 60 maybe they go to other places.
But wherever you're working, if someone asks-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], there were some, a few Hungarian, corp engineers.
They call then corp.
A lot of plain soldiers, plain.
It was terrible there.
Come in camp, bloody noses, bloody.
This I've seen myself.
But I--
We've been in Kiev about three, four days, I find myself-- there's nowhere to escape here.
I mean, can you image the escape?
I can escape, because I speak Ukraine, but where I'm going to escape in a city?
Now the sergeant who was in charge of our unit was a drunk man, but me considered that I am a poor accountant in the Carpathian, he made me like I was his servant also.
He was with me more less harsh. he did-- the other thing he was beating up, and [INAUDIBLE] still was this hanging, and all kinds.
I'm not going to tell.
OK, another story I forgot-- before we went to the Soviet-occupied territories, it was a Sunday, they allowed the women and men who had come to say good bye, but they didn't let them in.
They brought packages, they didn't let him in there.
This sergeant who got drunk-- I cannot go in all these stories.
So I'm in Kiev.
One day, there's a roll call and they take 30 people from the roll call where I am.
But this sergeant [NON-ENGLISH] tells that he wants-- he wants me, too.
They take me out on another roll call.
He wants me.
I should go with him.
So I go with him.
They line us up.
They walking.
We're going someplace.
I don't know where, where they take me.
Finally, the destination was [NON-ENGLISH] It's from Kiev not far, the first city, the first place.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was normal from World War I, there was a place that there are a lot of POWs there.
[NON-ENGLISH] was known even before.
I never heard about it by then.
And taking us though the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the river.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] River, that the Germans, the Russians destroyed the bridge, so they're building a bridge.
Shall I tell you what I have seen there?
We'll have to do that on the next tape.
Will stop now--
OK
